heading: BIBLIOGRAFIA 2849
card_summary: This chunk is a portion of the bibliography from Saverio Campanini's critical edition of Francesco Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi, covering entries from SCHOLEM through STELLA. It is dominated by two major scholarly presences: Gershom Scholem (works on Jewish mysticism, Kabbalah, and European intellectual history, published 1973-1995) and François Secret (prolific French scholar of Christian Kabbalah, with over thirty entries spanning 1956-1995 covering figures including Pico della Mirandola, Egidio da Viterbo, Guillaume Postel, Giulio Camillo Delminio, Flavius Mithridates, Johannes Reuchlin, and Franciscus Georgius Venetus himself). Additional entries address pilgrimage literature, adversus Judaeos texts, the Cambridge Platonists, Zoroastrianism, Hebrew bibliography and manuscript catalogues by Steinschneider, Venetian religious dissent, and the transmission of Neoplatonism and Hermeticism in the Renaissance.
essay:
# Bibliography Section: SCHOLEM through STELLA (pp. 3013–3019)

## Nature and Organization

This chunk presents a segment of the scholarly bibliography appended to Saverio Campanini's 2010 Bompiani critical edition of Francesco Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi totius*. The bibliography follows standard Italian critical-edition conventions: entries are arranged alphabetically by author surname, with multiple works by the same author listed chronologically under a repeated SURNAME + YEAR key (e.g., SECRET 1956, SECRET 1956a, SECRET 1957, etc.). Each entry gives full bibliographic data — author, title, journal or publisher, place, year — with parenthetical notes on original editions, Italian translations, and reprints where relevant. The section runs from Gershom Scholem to Aristide Stella, covering pages 3013–3019 in the volume's continuous pagination (which begins at 1 in the original Latin text and runs through the apparatus).

## Scholarly Value

The bibliography is an exceptionally rich resource for the study of Renaissance Christian Kabbalah and the intellectual world of Zorzi's Venice. Its value lies in several areas:

**François Secret's corpus as a research map.** The single most extensive block of entries belongs to François Secret (1911–2003), the foundational modern scholar of Christian Kabbalah. His entries here number over thirty, spanning 1956 to 1995, and collectively constitute a near-complete bibliography of his contributions to the field. They cover the major figures and themes of the Kabbalist Renaissance: Pico della Mirandola as the origin-point of Christian Kabbalah; Egidio da Viterbo's *Scechina* and Augustinian Neoplatonism; Guillaume Postel's prophetic hermeticism; Giulio Camillo Delminio's *Teatro del mondo*; the *Zohar* in Christian hands; Aristotle, Proclus, and the Chaldaean Oracles among Christian Kabbalists; and crucially, two entries (SECRET 1974a, SECRET 1974b) treating Franciscus Georgius Venetus — Zorzi himself — in relation to Proclus and the Chaldaean Oracles respectively, making them directly pertinent source-critical tools for the edition.

**Scholem's foundational scholarship.** The Scholem entries anchor the bibliography in the historiography of Jewish mysticism: *Kabbalah* (1974/Jerusalem), *Le origini della Kabbalà* (Bologna 1980, translated from the 1962 German via the 1966 French), the *Judaica* collections, and the essay on Kabbalah's place in European intellectual history (1983). These provide the Wissenschaftsgeschichte framework against which Campanini's edition situates Zorzi.

**Ancillary scholarly threads.** Beyond Secret and Scholem, this segment opens onto several supporting fields: Latin adversus Judaeos polemic (Schreckenberg), Kabbalist influence on the Cambridge Platonists Cudworth and More (Schulze), pilgrimage literature relating to the Holy Land (Schön, Schwab, Sommerfeld), Venetian evangelism and nicodemism (Simoncelli), Hebrew-Christian converts in the Renaissance (Simonsohn), medieval Hebrew bibliography and manuscript catalogues (multiple Steinschneider entries), John Donne's *Essays in Divinity* and *Catalogus Librorum* (Simpson), Zoroaster in early modern European religious history (Stausberg), ecclesiastical censorship in Italy (Stango), and Venetian Anabaptism and Socinianism (Stella). This breadth reflects the edition's ambition to situate Zorzi at the intersection of Jewish mysticism, Christian humanist theology, Neoplatonism, Hermeticism, and Venetian religious culture.

## Sample Entries (Verbatim)

**SCHOLEM 1980:** G. Scholem, *Le origini della Kabbalà*, Il Mulino, Bologna 1980 (ed. orig. Walter de Gruyter, Berlin 1962, ma questa traduzione fu condotta su quella francese, Aubier-Montaigne, Paris 1966).

**SECRET 1959a:** F. Secret, *Les cheminements de la Kabbale à la Renaissance. Le Théâtre du monde de Giulio Camillo Delminio et son influence*, in «Rivista critica di storia della filosofia» 14 (1959), pp. 419-437.

**SECRET 1974a:** F. Secret, *Franciscus Georgius Venetus et ses références à Proclus*, in «Bibliothèque d'Humanisme et Renaissance» 36 (1974), pp. 78-81.

**SECRET 1985:** F. Secret, *Les Kabbalistes chrétiens de la Renaissance*, Archè, Milano 1985² (ed. orig. Dunod, Paris 1964).

**STEINSCHNEIDER 1893:** M. Steinschneider, *Die hebräischen Übersetzungen des Mittelalters und die Juden als Dolmetscher*, Kommissionsverlag des bibliographischen Bureaus, Berlin 1893 [rist. anast. Akademische Druck- und Verlagsanstalt, Graz 1956].

## Conclusion

This bibliographic segment functions both as a scholarly apparatus for the edition's critical claims and as a self-contained research guide to the twentieth-century study of Renaissance Christian Kabbalah. The density of Secret entries in particular makes it an invaluable tool for anyone tracing the secondary literature on Zorzi's sources and contemporaries, while the Scholem cluster anchors it in the broader historiography of Jewish mysticism from which Christian Kabbalah draws and departs.
